nodes.name: Prequalification
nodes.desc: Vetting of a contractor or vendor's financial health, safety record, bonding capacity, and relevant experience BEFORE they are invited to bid. The gatekeeping step that determines who is eligible to compete for work. Evaluates EMR, TRIR, financials, references, and license status.

nodes.name: Bid Package
nodes.desc: A defined scope of work issued to qualified contractors for competitive pricing. Bundles drawings, specifications, schedule, and commercial terms into a single solicitation. The unit of procurement — one package per trade or work scope.

nodes.name: Request for Qualification
nodes.desc: Formal solicitation to assess contractor capabilities before inviting bids. Used on complex or specialized scopes where not all contractors can perform the work. Shortlists the field before pricing begins.

nodes.name: Request for Proposal
nodes.desc: Detailed solicitation requesting scope, approach, schedule, and price from qualified contractors. More comprehensive than a simple bid — evaluates methodology, not just price. Common on negotiated or best-value procurements.

nodes.name: Proposal / Bid
nodes.desc: A contractor's formal response to an ITB, RFQ, or RFP — includes pricing, scope clarifications, exclusions, alternates, and schedule. The document that becomes the basis for contract negotiation upon award.

nodes.name: Bid Evaluation / Leveling
nodes.desc: Comparative analysis of competing proposals — normalizing scope, pricing, qualifications, and exclusions to make an apples-to-apples recommendation. Identifies the apparent low bidder or best-value selection.

nodes.name: Award / Notice to Proceed
nodes.desc: Formal notification to the selected contractor that they have won the work and are authorized to begin. Triggers mobilization, procurement, and insurance/bonding requirements. May precede the executed contract via a Letter of Intent.

nodes.name: Bid Form
nodes.desc: The structured template a bidder fills in — defines what fields and line items the bid must include. One form per bid package. Standardizes bid comparison across vendors. Lifecycle: Created (with bid package) → Distributed → Submitted → Closed. Upstream: bid package. Downstream: bid items.

nodes.name: Bid Item
nodes.desc: A line item within a bid — the granular priced scope element. Sum of bid items = total bid amount. Enables line-level bid leveling and apples-to-apples vendor comparison. Key properties: description, quantity, unit, unit price, extended price. Upstream: bid form, parent bid. Downstream: post-award estimate budget items.

nodes.name: Bid Vendor
nodes.desc: A vendor invited to bid on a package — distinct from a vendor that submitted a bid (relationship: invited vs submitted vs awarded). Tracks bid coverage rate and bidder participation. Key properties: invitation date, response status, bid amount (if submitted), award status. Upstream: bid package, prequalification. Downstream: proposal/bid, award.

nodes.name: Subcontract
nodes.desc: Executed agreement between a general contractor and a subcontractor for a defined scope of work at an agreed price. The primary commercial vehicle for trade work. Includes scope, price, schedule, insurance, and flow-down terms.

nodes.name: Purchase Order
nodes.desc: Agreement for material supply, equipment rental, or services at a specified price. Simpler than a subcontract — typically no labor/scope-of-work component. Tracks what was ordered, from whom, at what cost.

nodes.name: Prime Contract
nodes.desc: Agreement between the project owner and the general contractor or CM. The top-level commercial agreement that defines the overall project price, scope, schedule, and terms. All subcontracts flow down from this.

nodes.name: Master Service Agreement
nodes.desc: Standing agreement with a vendor or subcontractor that covers multiple projects or a defined time period. Sets standard terms so individual project work orders can be issued without full contract negotiation each time.

nodes.name: Scope of Work
nodes.desc: Detailed description of contracted work — defines what the subcontractor or vendor is responsible for delivering. May reference drawings, specifications, and schedule. The single most important exhibit to any construction contract.

nodes.name: General Conditions
nodes.desc: The standard legal and administrative terms that apply to all contracts on a project — defines roles, responsibilities, dispute resolution, insurance requirements, and procedural obligations. The 'rules of the game' for the project.

nodes.name: Retainage Terms
nodes.desc: Contractual provision specifying the percentage of each payment withheld until substantial completion or final acceptance. Protects the owner/GC against incomplete or defective work. Typically 5-10% of contract value.

nodes.name: Liquidated Damages
nodes.desc: Pre-agreed daily or milestone-based financial penalty for late completion. Establishes the cost of delay without requiring the owner to prove actual damages. A critical risk term that drives schedule urgency.

nodes.name: Allowance
nodes.desc: A budgeted amount included in the contract for work that cannot be fully defined at contract execution. Spent against actual costs as scope is clarified. Commonly used for concealed conditions, owner selections, or design development items.

nodes.name: Bid Bond
nodes.desc: A surety bond submitted with a bid guaranteeing the bidder will enter into the contract at the bid price if awarded. Typically 5-10% of bid amount. Protects the owner against bidders who withdraw after bid opening.

nodes.name: Performance Bond
nodes.desc: Surety guarantee that the contractor will complete the work per contract terms. If the contractor defaults, the surety must complete the work or compensate the owner. Typically 100% of contract value.

nodes.name: Payment Bond
nodes.desc: Surety guarantee that the contractor will pay subcontractors, suppliers, and laborers. Protects the project from mechanic's liens filed by unpaid parties. Typically 100% of contract value.

nodes.name: Insurance Certificate
nodes.desc: Proof of insurance coverage — documents that the contractor carries required general liability, workers' comp, auto, umbrella, and builders risk coverage. Verified before work begins and tracked throughout the project.

nodes.name: Builders Risk
nodes.desc: Insurance policy covering physical loss or damage to the building under construction. Typically carried by the owner or GC and covers all parties. Separate from the contractor's general liability.

nodes.name: Warranty
nodes.desc: Post-construction guarantee that work will be free from defects for a specified period. Standard is 1 year from substantial completion; extended warranties apply to specific systems (roofing: 20 years, HVAC: 5 years, waterproofing: 10 years).

nodes.name: Substantial Completion
nodes.desc: The milestone when the project is sufficiently complete for the owner to occupy and use for its intended purpose. Triggers warranty start dates, retainage release, and final completion punchlist. A contractual — not just physical — milestone.

nodes.name: Final Completion
nodes.desc: The point at which ALL contract work is complete — all punch items resolved, all closeout documents delivered, all training completed. Triggers final retainage release and contract closeout. The true end of the project.

nodes.name: Prevailing Wage Certification
nodes.desc: Documentation proving that workers on public projects are paid at or above the locally determined prevailing wage rate. Required on federal (Davis-Bacon) and most state public work. Submitted with each pay period.

nodes.name: Diversity / DBE Certification
nodes.desc: Documentation of participation by disadvantaged, minority, women-owned, veteran-owned, or small businesses. Required on most public projects and increasingly on private work. Tracked as percentage of contract value.

nodes.name: Closeout Document Package
nodes.desc: The collection of documents required for project handover — as-builts, O&M manuals, warranties, training records, commissioning reports, and final lien waivers. The deliverable set that closes out the contract.

nodes.name: Bid Coverage Rate
nodes.desc: Percentage of project scope (by value) that was competitively bid vs. sole-sourced or negotiated. Formula: SUM(competitively_bid_contract_value) / total_contract_value. Higher rate = more price discovery.

nodes.name: Average Bidders per Package
nodes.desc: Mean number of competing proposals received per bid package. Formula: AVG(bidders_per_package). More bidders = more price competition and market depth.

nodes.name: Award Cycle Time
nodes.desc: Mean elapsed time from bid opening to contract execution. Formula: AVG(contract_date - bid_due_date). Shorter cycles get subs mobilized faster and reduce price escalation risk.

nodes.name: Insurance Compliance Rate
nodes.desc: Percentage of active subcontractors with current, compliant insurance certificates. Formula: COUNT(compliant_vendors) / COUNT(active_vendors). Below 100% = uninsured work exposure.

nodes.name: Subcontractor Retention Rate
nodes.desc: Percentage of subcontractors used on previous projects that are re-engaged on new projects. Formula: COUNT(repeat_subs) / COUNT(total_subs). Higher rate = stronger trade partner relationships.

nodes.name: Stipulated Sum / Lump Sum
nodes.desc: Fixed-price contract — contractor agrees to a single price for the full scope. AIA A101. Contractor takes scope and productivity risk. Owner takes design/scope-change risk only. Most common form for well-defined scopes. Variance = scope creep + estimating accuracy. Cost growth on stipulated sum = bad estimating or bad scope control.
nodes.derivation: finance.commitments WHERE single fixed grand_total, no hourly billing markers, no GMP attribute. Default contract type.

nodes.name: Cost Plus Fee
nodes.desc: Cost-reimbursable contract — owner pays actual cost plus a fee (fixed, fixed percentage, or sliding scale). AIA A102. Owner takes scope risk. Used for fast-track work, undefined scopes, emergency response. Fee structures: fixed fee (CPFF), percentage fee (CPPF), incentive fee (CPIF), award fee (CPAF). Higher administrative overhead — every cost line must be auditable.
nodes.derivation: finance.commitments WHERE line items have separate fee component (look for 'fee' / 'markup' / 'overhead' as line items separate from cost).

nodes.name: Cost Plus with GMP
nodes.desc: Hybrid: cost-plus reimbursement up to a Guaranteed Maximum Price (GMP) cap. AIA A102/A103. Owner takes scope risk up to the cap; contractor takes risk above. Often includes shared-savings clause: savings below GMP split per agreed percentage. Most common large-project structure where speed-to-start outweighs full-scope-definition needs.
nodes.derivation: finance.commitments WHERE GMP cap attribute exists, plus shared-savings clause indicators in commitment notes/description.

nodes.name: Guaranteed Maximum Price (GMP)
nodes.desc: The not-to-exceed price ceiling on a cost-plus contract. AIA A102/A103. Established at GMP conversion — typically when design reaches 60-90% complete. Original GMP, GMP changes (via owner-approved scope changes), revised GMP. GMP buster = projected cost > revised GMP, signaling need for scope cuts or contract restructure. Critical metric: GMP utilization % over time.
nodes.derivation: Project-level concept: total commitment + CE forecast capped at a known GMP value. Detect via fi-cost-forecast vs known-cap reference.

nodes.name: Time & Material (T&M)
nodes.desc: Hourly billing for labor + cost reimbursement for material, plus markup. Used for emergency, repair, and undefined-scope work. Highest cost risk for owner — no fixed price, no GMP cap (unless specifically negotiated). Each line is daily — labor hours × rate, materials at cost + markup, equipment at hourly/daily rate. Often capped with a not-to-exceed clause to limit exposure.
nodes.derivation: finance.commitments WHERE line items use rate × hours pattern, OR vendor appears in workforce.time_and_material_subcontractors. Higher cost-risk pattern.

nodes.name: Unit Price
nodes.desc: Per-unit pricing for variable-quantity work — $X per CY of excavation, $Y per LF of pipe, $Z per ton of asphalt. Common in civil/infrastructure contracts. Owner pays based on measured installed quantities. Risk allocation: contractor on unit production rate, owner on total quantity. Quantity overruns above a threshold (typically ±15%) often trigger renegotiation clauses.
nodes.derivation: finance.commitments WHERE line items follow unit_quantity × unit_price structure (commitment_line_items with quantity AND unit_price both populated).

nodes.name: Design-Bid-Build (DBB)
nodes.desc: Traditional procurement: owner contracts separately with designer (engineer/architect) and contractor. Design completes first, then bid, then build. Lowest first-cost path but longest duration; designer-contractor relationship is adversarial. Most common for public sector.

nodes.name: Design-Build (DB)
nodes.desc: Single contract with one entity for both design and construction. Faster, fewer disputes, single point of accountability. Risk is owner has less direct design control. Common for fast-track and design-build-friendly project types.

nodes.name: Construction Manager at Risk (CMAR)
nodes.desc: Owner contracts CM during design (consulting role) who then converts to GC at GMP for construction. Front-end constructability input + competitive subbidding + GMP cost cap. Common for complex commercial and institutional work.

nodes.name: Integrated Project Delivery (IPD)
nodes.desc: Multi-party contract among owner, architect, contractor (and sometimes key trades) with shared risk/reward and joint decision-making. Lean construction principles. Highest collaboration but requires mature parties and aligned incentives.

nodes.name: Public-Private Partnership (P3)
nodes.desc: Long-term contract where private party finances, designs, builds, operates, maintains a public asset for the public sector — recovers investment via tolls, availability payments, etc. Common for infrastructure (highways, bridges, schools, hospitals).
